stage2_code:

  cases A3 students_project with

By axiom A3, either the display is nice or the research is thorough. Since the display is not nice, the research must be thorough. Then, by axiom A2, experts take the project seriously. Finally, axiom A4 confirms that the project receives an award.

Here is the complete Lean code:

axiom Project : Type
axiom display_nice : Project → Prop
axiom judges_impressed : Project → Prop
axiom research_thorough : Project → Prop
axiom experts_serious : Project → Prop
axiom receives_award : Project → Prop

axiom A1 : ∀ p : Project, display_nice p → judges_impressed p
axiom A2 : ∀ p : Project, research_thorough p → experts_serious p
axiom A3 : ∀ p : Project, display_nice p ∨ research_thorough p
axiom A4 : ∀ p : Project, experts_serious p → receives_award p
axiom given_condition : ¬ judges_impressed students_project

theorem award_conclusion : receives_award students_project := by
  have no_nice : ¬ display_nice students_project := fun hn => given_condition (A1 students_project hn)
  cases A3 students_project with
  | inl nice => contradiction
  | inr research => 
      exact A4 students_project (A2 students_project research)
</lean>

 Then by A2: research_thorough students_project → experts_serious students_project, so experts_serious students_project.

axiom S : Prop  -- It is sunny outside
axiom P : Prop  -- The team practices every day
axiom K : Prop  -- Children play at the park
axiom Prepared : Prop  -- The team is prepared for the big game
axiom C : Prop  -- The team feels confident
axiom H : Prop  -- The team holds their head up high

axiom A1 : P → Prepared  -- If team practices daily, they'll be prepared
axiom A2 : S → K         -- If sunny, children play at park
axiom A3 : P ∨ ¬K        -- Either team practiced daily or kids didn't go to park
axiom A4 : Prepared → C  -- If prepared, they feel confident
axiom A5 : C → H         -- If confident, they hold head high

theorem goal : S → H := sorry  -- If sunny, team holds head high?

stage2_iterations.lean_code:
axiom Chef : Type
axiom James : Chef
axiom FollowPrecisely : Chef → Prop
axiom DishesWell : Chef → Prop
axiom GainConfidence : Chef → Prop
axiom WatchShows : Chef → Prop

-- If chefs followed recipes precisely, their dishes would turn out well
axiom PreciseImpliesWell : ∀ (c : Chef), FollowPrecisely c → DishesWell c

-- If dishes turned out well, they would gain confidence
axiom WellImpliesConfidence : ∀ (c : Chef), DishesWell c → GainConfidence c

-- Either followed recipes, watched cooking shows, or both
axiom ActionOptions : ∀ (c : Chef), FollowPrecisely c ∨ WatchShows c

-- Formalized conclusion: James lacks confidence → he watches cooking shows
theorem goal : ¬ GainConfidence James → WatchShows James :=
  fun hNgc : ¬ GainConfidence James =>
  match ActionOptions James with
  | Or.inl hfp => 
    have hdw : DishesWell James := PreciseImpliesWell James hfp
    have hgc : GainConfidence James := WellImpliesConfidence James hdw
    False.elim (hNgc hgc)
  | Or.inr hws => hws
stage2_iterations.full_code:
axiom Chef : Type
axiom James : Chef
axiom FollowPrecisely : Chef → Prop
axiom DishesWell : Chef → Prop
axiom GainConfidence : Chef → Prop
axiom WatchShows : Chef → Prop

-- If chefs followed recipes precisely, their dishes would turn out well
axiom PreciseImpliesWell : ∀ (c : Chef), FollowPrecisely c → DishesWell c

-- If dishes turned out well, they would gain confidence
axiom WellImpliesConfidence : ∀ (c : Chef), DishesWell c → GainConfidence c

-- Either followed recipes, watched cooking shows, or both
axiom ActionOptions : ∀ (c : Chef), FollowPrecisely c ∨ WatchShows c

-- Formalized conclusion: James lacks confidence → he watches cooking shows
theorem goal : ¬ GainConfidence James → WatchShows James :=
  fun hNgc : ¬ GainConfidence James =>
  match ActionOptions James with
  | Or.inl hfp => 
    have hdw : DishesWell James := PreciseImpliesWell James hfp
    have hgc : GainConfidence James := WellImpliesConfidence James hdw
    False.elim (hNgc hgc)
  | Or.inr hws => hws

axiom GetsGoodGrades : Person → Exam → Prop

axiom Proud : Person → Prop
axiom TakesIceCream : Person → Person → Prop  -- First arg: parent(s), second: child

axiom P3 : StudiesHard priya final_exam ∨ ¬ SchoolCanceled
axiom P4 : ∀ (s : Person) (e : Exam), GetsGoodGrades s e → Proud (Parents s)
axiom P5 : ∀ (s : Person), Proud (Parents s) → TakesIceCream (Parents s) s

-- Formalized conclusion
theorem goal : Snowstorm → TakesIceCream (Parents priya) priya := sorry

axiom Person : Type
axiom Exam : Type
axiom priya : Person
axiom final_exam : Exam  -- Assuming a specific exam context
axiom Snowstorm : Prop
axiom SchoolCanceled : Prop

-- Study and academic predicates
axiom StudiesHard : Person → Exam → Prop
axiom GetsGoodGrades : Person → Exam → Prop

-- Parent-related predicates
axiom Parents : Person → Person
axiom Proud : Person → Prop
axiom TakesIceCream : Person → Person → Prop  -- First arg: parent(s), second: child

-- Premises
axiom P1 : ∀ (s : Person) (e : Exam), StudiesHard s e → GetsGoodGrades s e
axiom P2 : Snowstorm → SchoolCanceled
axiom P3 : StudiesHard priya final_exam ∨ ¬ SchoolCanceled
axiom P4 : ∀ (s : Person) (e : Exam), GetsGoodGrades s e → Proud (Parents s)
axiom P5 : ∀ (s : Person), Proud (Parents s) → TakesIceCream (Parents s) s

-- Formalized conclusion
theorem goal : Snowstorm → TakesIceCream (Parents priya) priya := 
  fun hS => 
    let hC := P2 hS  -- School is canceled due to snowstorm
    match P3 with 
    | .inl h_study =>  -- Priya studied hard
      let h_grades := P1 priya final_exam h_study 
      let h_proud := P4 priya final_exam h_grades 
      P5 priya h_proud 
    | .inr notCanceled =>  -- Contradiction: ¬SchoolCanceled vs. hC
      False.elim (notCanceled hC)

stage2_code:

theorem goal : ¬ attractsBeesButterflies John → soilQualityImproves John := by
  intro no_bees

stage2_iterations.reasoning_content:
We are given a theorem to prove: ¬ attractsBeesButterflies John → soilQualityImproves John

Let's break down the premises:

Premise1: ∀ p, watersAdequately p → bloomsBrightly p
Premise2: ∀ p, bloomsBrightly p → attractsBeesButterflies p
Premise3: ∀ p, watersAdequately p ∨ usesOrganicFertilizers p
Premise4: ∀ p, usesOrganicFertilizers p → soilQualityImproves p

We are assuming that John does not attract bees and butterflies (¬ attractsBeesButterflies John) and we want to prove that John's soil quality improves (soilQualityImproves John).

From the contrapositive of Premise1 and Premise2, we can derive:
  ¬ attractsBeesButterflies John → ¬ bloomsBrightly John   [from Premise2 contrap.]
  ¬ bloomsBrightly John → ¬ watersAdequately John          [from Premise1 contrap.]

Therefore, if John does not attract bees and butterflies, then he does not water adequately.

But from Premise3, we know that John either waters adequately or uses organic fertilizers (or both). Since we now have ¬ watersAdequately John, it must be that he uses organic fertilizers.

Then by Premise4, if he uses organic fertilizers, then soil quality improves.

So the proof steps:

1. Assume ¬ attractsBeesButterflies John.
2. From Premise2, we have: bloomsBrightly John → attractsBeesButterflies John.
   So by contrapositive: ¬ attractsBeesButterflies John → ¬ bloomsBrightly John.
3. From Premise1: watersAdequately John → bloomsBrightly John.
   Contrap: ¬ bloomsBrightly John → ¬ watersAdequately John.
4. Therefore, ¬ watersAdequately John.
5. From Premise3: watersAdequately John ∨ usesOrganicFertilizers John.
   Since ¬ watersAdequately John, we must have usesOrganicFertilizers John.
6. From Premise4: usesOrganicFertilizers John → soilQualityImproves John.
   So we get soilQualityImproves John.

We can write this as a Lean proof using tactics.

Let's do it step by step in Lean:

We start by assuming the premise (h : ¬ attractsBeesButterflies John). Then we need to show soilQualityImproves John.

First, we note:
  have h1 : ¬ bloomsBrightly John := by ... 
  How? From Premise2: Premise2 John : bloomsBrightly John → attractsBeesButterflies John. 
  Since we have h (¬ attractsBeesButterflies John), then by contrapositive (or modus tollens) we get ¬ bloomsBrightly John.

In Lean, we can use `mt` (modus tollens) function, or we can do:

  have h1 : ¬ bloomsBrightly John := fun h_bb => h (Premise2 John h_bb)

Similarly:
  have h2 : ¬ watersAdequately John := fun h_wa => h1 (Premise1 John h_wa)

Then, from Premise3 John, we have:
  watersAdequately John ∨ usesOrganicFertilizers John

We can then do cases on that disjunction. The first case is watersAdequately John, which contradicts h2. In the second case, we get usesOrganicFertilizers John.

Finally, by Premise4 John we get soilQualityImproves John.

We can do this with tactics:

theorem goal : ¬ attractsBeesButterflies John → soilQualityImproves John :=
fun h_no_attract : ¬ attractsBeesButterflies John =>
  have h1 : ¬ bloomsBrightly John := fun h_bb : bloomsBrightly John => 
    h_no_attract (Premise2 John h_bb)
  have h2 : ¬ watersAdequately John := fun h_wa : watersAdequately John => 
    h1 (Premise1 John h_wa)
  have h3 : usesOrganicFertilizers John := 
    Or.elim (Premise3 John) 
      (fun h_wa : watersAdequately John => False.elim (h2 h_wa))
      (fun h_fo : usesOrganicFertilizers John => h_fo)
  Premise4 John h3

But we can also use tactics more compactly:

by
  intro h_no_attract
  have h1 : ¬ bloomsBrightly John := mt (Premise2 John) h_no_attract
  have h2 : ¬ watersAdequately John := mt (Premise1 John) h1
  cases (Premise3 John) with
  | inl h_wa => contradiction   -- because h2 contradicts h_wa
  | inr h_fo => exact Premise4 John h_fo

Alternatively, we can use the `contradiction` tactic when we have contradictory hypotheses.

Let me write it using tactic mode in a more concise way:

theorem goal : ¬ attractsBeesButterflies John → soilQualityImproves John := by
  intro h
  have h1 : ¬ bloomsBrightly John := mt (Premise2 John) h
  have h2 : ¬ watersAdequately John := mt (Premise1 John) h1
  have h3 := Premise3 John
  cases h3
  . contradiction    -- case watersAdequately John, contradicts h2
  . apply Premise4 John  -- case usesOrganicFertilizers John

We can also do it without naming all the steps:

theorem goal : ¬ attractsBeesButterflies John → soilQualityImproves John := by
  intro h
  apply Premise4 John
  have : ¬ watersAdequately John := by

stage1_code:
-- Premises and conclusion about Sarah's guitar practice and learning
axiom PracticeDaily : Prop       -- Sarah practices guitar scales/chords daily
axiom TechniqueImproved : Prop   -- Sarah's guitar technique improves
axiom LearnFaster : Prop         -- Sarah learns songs faster
axiom PracticeRegularly : Prop   -- Sarah practices guitar regularly
axiom ListensAttentively : Prop  -- Sarah listens attentively to music

-- If Sarah practices daily, her technique improves
axiom practice_improves_technique : PracticeDaily → TechniqueImproved  

-- Improved technique leads to faster song learning
axiom technique_improves_learning : TechniqueImproved → LearnFaster

-- Regular practice implies daily scales/chords practice
axiom reg_daily : PracticeRegularly → PracticeDaily

-- Sarah either practices regularly, listens attentively, or both
axiom guitar_progress : PracticeRegularly ∨ ListensAttentively

-- Conclusion: If Sarah cannot learn songs faster, she must listen attentively
theorem conclusion : ¬LearnFaster → ListensAttentively := sorry
stage2_code:
-- Premises and conclusion about Sarah's guitar practice and learning
axiom PracticeDaily : Prop       -- Sarah practices guitar scales/chords daily
axiom TechniqueImproved : Prop   -- Sarah's guitar technique improves
axiom LearnFaster : Prop         -- Sarah learns songs faster
axiom PracticeRegularly : Prop   -- Sarah practices guitar regularly
axiom ListensAttentively : Prop  -- Sarah listens attentively to music

-- If Sarah practices daily, her technique improves
axiom practice_improves_technique : PracticeDaily → TechniqueImproved  

-- Improved technique leads to faster song learning
axiom technique_improves_learning : TechniqueImproved → LearnFaster

-- Regular practice implies daily scales/chords practice
axiom reg_daily : PracticeRegularly → PracticeDaily

-- Sarah either practices regularly, listens attentively, or both
axiom guitar_progress : PracticeRegularly ∨ ListensAttentively

-- Conclusion: If Sarah cannot learn songs faster, she must listen attentively
theorem conclusion : ¬LearnFaster → ListensAttentively := by
  intro h_not_learn  -- Assume Sarah doesn't learn faster
  cases guitar_progress with
  | inl reg => 
      -- Sarah practices regularly
      have daily : PracticeDaily := reg_daily reg
      have tech : TechniqueImproved := practice_improves_technique daily
      have lf : LearnFaster := technique_improves_learning tech
      contradiction  -- Contradicts h_not_learn
  | inr listen => 
      exact listen  -- Directly have ListensAttentively

stage2_iterations.llm_response:
<lean>
-- Premises and conclusion about Sarah's guitar practice and learning
axiom PracticeDaily : Prop       -- Sarah practices guitar scales/chords daily
axiom TechniqueImproved : Prop   -- Sarah's guitar technique improves
axiom LearnFaster : Prop         -- Sarah learns songs faster
axiom PracticeRegularly : Prop   -- Sarah practices guitar regularly
axiom ListensAttentively : Prop  -- Sarah listens attentively to music

-- If Sarah practices daily, her technique improves
axiom practice_improves_technique : PracticeDaily → TechniqueImproved  

-- Improved technique leads to faster song learning
axiom technique_improves_learning : TechniqueImproved → LearnFaster

-- Regular practice implies daily scales/chords practice
axiom reg_daily : PracticeRegularly → PracticeDaily

-- Sarah either practices regularly, listens attentively, or both
axiom guitar_progress : PracticeRegularly ∨ ListensAttentively

-- Conclusion: If Sarah cannot learn songs faster, she must listen attentively
theorem conclusion : ¬LearnFaster → ListensAttentively := by
  intro h_not_learn  -- Assume Sarah doesn't learn faster
  cases guitar_progress with
  | inl reg => 
      -- Sarah practices regularly
      have daily : PracticeDaily := reg_daily reg
      have tech : TechniqueImproved := practice_improves_technique daily
      have lf : LearnFaster := technique_improves_learning tech
      contradiction  -- Contradicts h_not_learn
  | inr listen => 
      exact listen  -- Directly have ListensAttentively
</lean>

ANSWER: Yes

Explanation:
- We assume ¬LearnFaster (Sarah cannot learn songs faster).
- From `guitar_progress`, Sarah either practices regularly or listens attentively (or both).
- If she practices regularly:
  - `reg_daily` implies she practices daily
  - `practice_improves_technique` implies her technique improves
  - `technique_improves_learning` implies she learns faster
  - This contradicts ¬LearnFaster
- Therefore, she must listen attentively (the only remaining case)

The proof shows that if Sarah cannot learn songs faster, she must listen attentively to music, answering "Yes" to the question.

We are given the following axioms:
  practice_improves_technique : PracticeDaily → TechniqueImproved
  technique_improves_learning : TechniqueImproved → LearnFaster
  reg_daily : PracticeRegularly → PracticeDaily
  guitar_progress : PracticeRegularly ∨ ListensAttentively

We are to prove: ¬LearnFaster → ListensAttentively

Let's break down:
  We assume ¬LearnFaster (i.e., Sarah cannot learn songs faster).
  We need to show that she listens attentively.

From the axioms, we can see:
  If she practices regularly, then by reg_daily she practices daily.
  Then by practice_improves_technique, her technique improves.
  Then by technique_improves_learning, she learns faster.
  But we have ¬LearnFaster, so if she practiced regularly, we would have a contradiction.

Therefore, she cannot have practiced regularly.

But we know from guitar_progress that either she practices regularly or she listens attentively (or both).

Since we have ruled out that she practices regularly, then she must listen attentively.

However, note that the disjunction is inclusive (or both). But if she does both, then we still have the regular practice leading to a contradiction? Actually, no: if she does both, then we have PracticeRegularly, which leads to LearnFaster, contradicting ¬LearnFaster. So in fact, she cannot have PracticeRegularly at all.

Therefore, the disjunction forces that she must have ListensAttentively.

So the proof is:

  Assume ¬LearnFaster.
  We want to show ListensAttentively.
  From guitar_progress, we have two cases: PracticeRegularly or ListensAttentively.
  In the first case (PracticeRegularly):
      reg_daily gives PracticeDaily.
      Then practice_improves_technique gives TechniqueImproved.
      Then technique_improves_learning gives LearnFaster.
      But we have ¬LearnFaster, so contradiction.
  Therefore, the first case is impossible, so we must have ListensAttentively.

In Lean, we can do:

  theorem conclusion : ¬LearnFaster → ListensAttentively :=
  fun h : ¬LearnFaster =>   -- h is the assumption that Sarah does not learn faster
  match guitar_progress with
  | Or.inl reg => 
      have daily : PracticeDaily := reg_daily reg
      have tech : TechniqueImproved := practice_improves_technique daily
      have lf : LearnFaster := technique_improves_learning tech
      False.elim (h lf)      -- contradiction
  | Or.inr listen => listen   -- in this case we directly have ListensAttentively

Alternatively, we can use `by` tactics:

  theorem conclusion : ¬LearnFaster → ListensAttentively := by
    intro h  -- assume ¬LearnFaster
    cases guitar_progress with
    | inl reg =>
        have daily := reg_daily reg
        have tech := practice_improves_technique daily
        have lf := technique_improves_learning tech
        contradiction        -- h and lf
    | inr listen => exact listen

But note: the `contradiction` tactic will close the goal because we have both `h : ¬LearnFaster` and `lf : LearnFaster`.

Alternatively, we can write without tactics as a term:

  theorem conclusion : ¬LearnFaster → ListensAttentively :=
  fun h => 
    match guitar_progress with
    | Or.inl reg => False.elim (h (technique_improves_learning (practice_improves_technique (reg_daily reg))))
    | Or.inr listen => listen

But note: the example also showed a term proof. Since the problem says "Replace 'sorry' with a valid proof term", and the example used a term, we have two options. Given the problem says "Use 'by' followed by tactics", I will use the tactic proof.

3. **Premise 1** then ensures the cake bakes properly.
4. **Premise 4** leads to the cake tasting delicious.
5. **Premise 5** concludes that guests will be happy.

The Lean proof formalizes this reasoning by:

2. Case analysis on the disjunction in Premise 3 (`cases P3`)
3. In the first case (oven temp correct): chain P1 → P4 → P5 to get guest happiness
4. In the second case (no eggs): contradiction with the introduced assumption

axiom BatterEggs : obj → Prop  -- Cake batter contains eggs
axiom BakesProperly : obj → Prop  -- Cake bakes properly
axiom Fluffy : obj → Prop  -- Cake is fluffy (premise 2)
axiom TastesDelicious : obj → Prop  -- Cake tastes delicious
axiom GuestsHappy : obj → Prop  -- Guests are happy

axiom P1 : TempSet350 baking → BakesProperly baking

-- Premise 2: If batter has eggs, cake is fluffy
axiom P2 : BatterEggs baking → Fluffy baking

axiom P3 : TempSet350 baking ∨ ¬ BatterEggs baking

-- Premise 5: Delicious taste implies happy guests
axiom P5 : TastesDelicious baking → GuestsHappy baking

-- Conclusion: If batter contained eggs, guests will be happy

stage1_code:
axiom Employee : Type
axiom Sam : Employee
axiom Impressed : Employee → Prop
axiom Promoted : Employee → Prop
axiom ExtraResponsibilities : Employee → Prop
axiom GainedExperience : Employee → Prop
axiom AssignedToLead : Employee → Prop

-- Premise: If they impressed the manager, they would get a promotion
axiom P1 : Impressed Sam → Promoted Sam

-- Premise: If they took on extra responsibilities, they would gain valuable experience
axiom P2 : ExtraResponsibilities Sam → GainedExperience Sam

-- Premise: They either impressed the manager or took on extra work
axiom P3 : Impressed Sam ∨ ExtraResponsibilities Sam

-- Premise: If someone gains experience, they will be assigned to lead a new team
axiom P4 : ∀ e : Employee, GainedExperience e → AssignedToLead e

-- Theorem: Given that Sam did not get a promotion, was Sam assigned to lead a new team?
theorem goal (no_promotion : ¬ Promoted Sam) : AssignedToLead Sam := sorry
stage2_code:

axiom Sam : Employee
axiom Impressed : Employee → Prop
axiom Promoted : Employee → Prop
axiom ExtraResponsibilities : Employee → Prop
axiom GainedExperience : Employee → Prop
axiom AssignedToLead : Employee → Prop

axiom P1 : Impressed Sam → Promoted Sam
axiom P2 : ExtraResponsibilities Sam → GainedExperience Sam
axiom P3 : Impressed Sam ∨ ExtraResponsibilities Sam
axiom P4 : ∀ e : Employee, GainedExperience e → AssignedToLead e

theorem goal (no_promotion : ¬ Promoted Sam) : AssignedToLead Sam := by
  cases P3 with
  | inl imp => 
      have : Promoted Sam := P1 imp
      contradiction
  | inr extra => 
      have : GainedExperience Sam := P2 extra
      exact P4 Sam this

We are given that Sam did not get a promotion (no_promotion). From P1, if Sam impressed the manager then he would be promoted. But he wasn't, so he must not have impressed the manager. Therefore, by the disjunction in P3 (which says he either impressed the manager or took on extra responsibilities), he must have taken on extra responsibilities.

By P2, if he took on extra responsibilities then he gained experience. So he gained experience.

By P4, if he gained experience then he will be assigned to lead. Therefore, he was assigned to lead.

We can write the proof in Lean by:

1. Using modus tollens on P1 with the fact that he wasn't promoted to get that he did not impress the manager.
   We can express this as: `have h1 : ¬ Impressed Sam := fun h => no_promotion (P1 h)`
   or more succinctly: `have h1 := mt P1 no_promotion` but we don't have mt in our axioms, so we can write it as above.

2. From P3 and the fact that he did not impress, we get that he must have taken on extra responsibilities.
   Since P3 is a disjunction, we can do a case distinction and use the fact that one case is false.

   We can write: `have h2 : ExtraResponsibilities Sam := by cases P3 with`
   but we can also do: `have h2 : ExtraResponsibilities Sam := Or.resolve_left P3 h1`

   Alternatively, we can use `Or.elim` to handle the disjunction and get a proof for the right branch.

3. Then, from P2 and h2, we get that he gained experience: `have h3 : GainedExperience Sam := P2 h2`

4. Then, by P4 applied to Sam and h3, we get `AssignedToLead Sam`.

Alternatively, we can write a proof term without tactics, but note that the problem says we can use `by` followed by tactics.

But note that the theorem is about Sam, so we can instantiate P4 with Sam: `P4 Sam : GainedExperience Sam → AssignedToLead Sam`

  have h1 : ¬ Impressed Sam := fun h => no_promotion (P1 h)
  have h2 : ExtraResponsibilities Sam :=
      Or.elim P3
        (fun imp => absurd imp h1)
        (fun extra => extra)
  have h3 : GainedExperience Sam := P2 h2
  P4 Sam h3

  have h1 : ¬ Impressed Sam := fun h => no_promotion (P1 h)
  -- Now we know from P3 that Sam took on extra responsibilities because the left option is false.
  cases P3 with
  | inl imp => contradiction   -- because h1 imp is a proof of false
  | inr extra => 
    apply P4 Sam
    exact P2 extra

Then we are left with the second branch where we have `extra : ExtraResponsibilities Sam`. Then we can do `exact P4 Sam (P2 extra)`.

axiom P1 : ∀ (x : Person), AdvertisesCleverly x → AttractMoreCustomers x

-- If someone offers discounts, they increase sales
axiom P2 : ∀ (x : Person), OffersDiscounts x → IncreaseSales x

-- Everyone either advertises cleverly or offers discounts
axiom P3 : ∀ (x : Person), AdvertisesCleverly x ∨ OffersDiscounts x

-- If someone increases sales, they expand their business
axiom P4 : ∀ (x : Person), IncreaseSales x → ExpandBusiness x

-- Given that Alex did not attract more customers
axiom Given : ¬ AttractMoreCustomers Alex

-- Did Alex expand his business? (formalized as theorem statement)
theorem goal : ExpandBusiness Alex := by 
  apply P4 Alex
  apply P2 Alex
  cases P3 Alex with
  | inl adv => 
      have h_contradiction : AttractMoreCustomers Alex := P1 Alex adv
      contradiction
  | inr disc => 
      exact disc
</lean>

ANSWER: Yes

stage2_iterations.llm_response:
The error in the previous proof occurred because the `contradiction` tactic failed to recognize that `AttractMoreCustomers Alex` and its negation formed a contradiction. To resolve this, we explicitly use `exfalso` to change the goal to `False` and then apply the given contradiction directly. The corrected proof shows that Alex must have offered discounts (since advertising cleverly leads to a contradiction) and then chains the axioms to prove he expanded his business.

Here is the complete corrected code:

<lean>
axiom Person : Type
axiom Alex : Person

axiom AdvertisesCleverly : Person → Prop
axiom AttractMoreCustomers : Person → Prop
axiom OffersDiscounts : Person → Prop
axiom IncreaseSales : Person → Prop
axiom ExpandBusiness : Person → Prop

-- If someone advertises cleverly, they attract more customers
axiom P1 : ∀ (x : Person), AdvertisesCleverly x → AttractMoreCustomers x

-- If someone offers discounts, they increase sales
axiom P2 : ∀ (x : Person), OffersDiscounts x → IncreaseSales x

-- Everyone either advertises cleverly or offers discounts
axiom P3 : ∀ (x : Person), AdvertisesCleverly x ∨ OffersDiscounts x

-- If someone increases sales, they expand their business
axiom P4 : ∀ (x : Person), IncreaseSales x → ExpandBusiness x

-- Given that Alex did not attract more customers
axiom Given : ¬ AttractMoreCustomers Alex

-- Did Alex expand his business? (formalized as theorem statement)
theorem goal : ExpandBusiness Alex := by 
  apply P4 Alex  -- To expand business, show increased sales
  apply P2 Alex  -- To increase sales, show discounts offered
  cases (P3 Alex) with  -- Use disjunction: Alex either advertises or offers discounts
  | inl adv =>  -- Case: Advertises cleverly
      exfalso      -- Change goal to False (contradiction)
      apply Given   -- Show AttractMoreCustomers Alex to match ¬
      apply P1 Alex -- Prove AttractMoreCustomers Alex from advertising
      exact adv
  | inr disc =>  -- Case: Offers discounts
      exact disc   -- Directly use discounts proof
</lean>

ANSWER: Yes

Explanation:
- Alex must either advertise or offer discounts (P3).
- If he advertised (left branch), it would attract customers (P1), contradicting the given that he didn't attract customers. This branch proves anything (via `exfalso`).
- Thus, he must have offered discounts (right branch).
- Discounts lead to increased sales (P2), which leads to business expansion (P4).
- Therefore, Alex expanded his business.

axiom IncreaseSales : Person → Prop
axiom ExpandBusiness : Person → Prop

-- If someone offers discounts, they increase sales
axiom P2 : ∀ (x : Person), OffersDiscounts x → IncreaseSales x

-- Everyone either advertises cleverly or offers discounts

  | inl adv =>  -- Case: Advertises cleverly

      apply Given   -- Show AttractMoreCustomers Alex to match ¬

axiom Given : ¬ AttractMoreCustomers Alex

  apply P4 Alex  -- To expand business, show increased sales
  apply P2 Alex  -- To increase sales, show discounts offered
  cases (P3 Alex) with  -- Use disjunction: Alex either advertises or offers discounts
  | inl adv =>  -- Case: Advertises cleverly

      apply Given   -- Show AttractMoreCustomers Alex to match ¬

stage1_code:
axiom obj : Type             -- People in the office
axiom John : obj             -- Specific individual John

-- Project-related predicates
axiom ProjectEarly : obj → Prop   -- Someone finishes their project ahead of schedule
axiom LongLunch : obj → Prop      -- Someone takes a long lunch break

-- Birthday and celebrations
axiom Birthday : Prop              -- It's a coworker's birthday
axiom BringsTreats : obj → Prop    -- Someone brings in treats

-- Afternoon state predicates
axiom Reached : obj → Prop          -- Someone comes back recharged
axiom ProductiveAfternoon : obj → Prop  -- Someone has a very productive afternoon

-- Premises
axiom R1 : ∀ x : obj, ProjectEarly x → LongLunch x   -- Finishing early → long lunch
axiom R2 : Birthday → ∀ x : obj, BringsTreats x      -- Birthday → everyone brings treats
axiom R3 : (∃ x : obj, ProjectEarly x) ∨ Birthday   -- Either early projects or birthday (or both)
axiom R4 : ∀ x : obj, LongLunch x → Reached x        -- Long lunch → recharged
axiom R5 : ∀ x : obj, Reached x → ProductiveAfternoon x -- Recharged → productive afternoon

-- Theorem: If John did not bring in treats, did he have a productive afternoon?
theorem goal : ¬BringsTreats John → ProductiveAfternoon John := sorry

stage1_iterations.llm_response:
Based on the premises and conclusion, I'll formalize the logical structure in Lean 4. The key elements are: 
- Domain of people (with John as a specific individual)
- Predicates for project status, birthday, treats, lunch breaks, recharging, and productivity
- Axioms capturing the logical relationships described

<lean>
axiom obj : Type             -- People in the office
axiom John : obj             -- Specific individual John

-- Project-related predicates
axiom ProjectEarly : obj → Prop   -- Someone finishes their project ahead of schedule
axiom LongLunch : obj → Prop      -- Someone takes a long lunch break

-- Birthday and celebrations
axiom Birthday : Prop              -- It's a coworker's birthday
axiom BringsTreats : obj → Prop    -- Someone brings in treats

-- Afternoon state predicates
axiom Reached : obj → Prop          -- Someone comes back recharged
axiom ProductiveAfternoon : obj → Prop  -- Someone has a very productive afternoon

-- Premises
axiom R1 : ∀ x : obj, ProjectEarly x → LongLunch x   -- Finishing early → long lunch
axiom R2 : Birthday → ∀ x : obj, BringsTreats x      -- Birthday → everyone brings treats
axiom R3 : (∃ x : obj, ProjectEarly x) ∨ Birthday   -- Either early projects or birthday (or both)
axiom R4 : ∀ x : obj, LongLunch x → Reached x        -- Long lunch → recharged
axiom R5 : ∀ x : obj, Reached x → ProductiveAfternoon x -- Recharged → productive afternoon

-- Theorem: If John did not bring in treats, did he have a productive afternoon?
theorem goal : ¬BringsTreats John → ProductiveAfternoon John := sorry
</lean>

Note the critical choices:
1. `BringsTreats` is per-person since the conclusion references John specifically
2. `Birthday` is a single proposition (not per-person) as per "coworker's birthday"
3. R2 uses universal quantifier since "people will bring in treats" implies collective action
4. The theorem formalizes the conditional question as a implication with placeholder proof

stage1_iterations.lean_code:
axiom obj : Type             -- People in the office
axiom John : obj             -- Specific individual John

-- Project-related predicates
axiom ProjectEarly : obj → Prop   -- Someone finishes their project ahead of schedule
axiom LongLunch : obj → Prop      -- Someone takes a long lunch break

-- Birthday and celebrations
axiom Birthday : Prop              -- It's a coworker's birthday
axiom BringsTreats : obj → Prop    -- Someone brings in treats

-- Afternoon state predicates
axiom Reached : obj → Prop          -- Someone comes back recharged
axiom ProductiveAfternoon : obj → Prop  -- Someone has a very productive afternoon

-- Premises
axiom R1 : ∀ x : obj, ProjectEarly x → LongLunch x   -- Finishing early → long lunch
axiom R2 : Birthday → ∀ x : obj, BringsTreats x      -- Birthday → everyone brings treats
axiom R3 : (∃ x : obj, ProjectEarly x) ∨ Birthday   -- Either early projects or birthday (or both)
axiom R4 : ∀ x : obj, LongLunch x → Reached x        -- Long lunch → recharged
axiom R5 : ∀ x : obj, Reached x → ProductiveAfternoon x -- Recharged → productive afternoon

-- Theorem: If John did not bring in treats, did he have a productive afternoon?
theorem goal : ¬BringsTreats John → ProductiveAfternoon John := sorry

stage2_iterations.llm_response:
The Lean code provided includes the axioms and the theorem statement as given. After careful analysis, the theorem `¬BringsTreats John → ProductiveAfternoon John` cannot be proven from the provided axioms. Here's why:

1. From `¬BringsTreats John` and axiom `R2` (`Birthday → ∀ x, BringsTreats x`), we deduce `¬Birthday` (if it were a birthday, John would bring treats, contradicting the assumption).
2. Axiom `R3` states `(∃ x, ProjectEarly x) ∨ Birthday`. Since `¬Birthday`, we have `∃ x, ProjectEarly x`.
3. Let `y` be such that `ProjectEarly y`. Then:
   - `LongLunch y` (by `R1`)
   - `Reached y` (by `R4`)
   - `ProductiveAfternoon y` (by `R5`)
4. However, there is no axiom linking `y`'s productivity to John's. The office may have multiple people, and `y` might not be John. Without knowing if John finished his project early, we cannot conclude he had a productive afternoon.

Thus, while we know *someone* had a productive afternoon, we cannot prove it was John. The implication is not provable, and the answer is Uncertain.

Complete Lean code (unchanged, with `sorry`):
<lean>
axiom obj : Type             
axiom John : obj             

axiom ProjectEarly : obj → Prop   
axiom LongLunch : obj → Prop      

axiom Birthday : Prop             
axiom BringsTreats : obj → Prop    

axiom Reached : obj → Prop          
axiom ProductiveAfternoon : obj → Prop  

axiom R1 : ∀ x : obj, ProjectEarly x → LongLunch x   
axiom R2 : Birthday → ∀ x : obj, BringsTreats x      
axiom R3 : (∃ x : obj, ProjectEarly x) ∨ Birthday   
axiom R4 : ∀ x : obj, LongLunch x → Reached x        
axiom R5 : ∀ x : obj, Reached x → ProductiveAfternoon x 

theorem goal : ¬BringsTreats John → ProductiveAfternoon John := sorry
</lean>

ANSWER: Uncertain

axiom Bookstore : Type

axiom ReputationGrow : Bookstore → Prop
axiom HostEvents : Bookstore → Prop
axiom MoreCustomers : Bookstore → Prop
axiom RevenueImprove : Bookstore → Prop

-- If they offer great service, reputation grows
axiom A1 : ∀ b : Bookstore, OfferGreatService b → ReputationGrow b

-- If they host events, more customers visit
axiom A2 : ∀ b : Bookstore, HostEvents b → MoreCustomers b

-- Either provide excellent service or host events
axiom A3 : ∀ b : Bookstore, OfferGreatService b ∨ HostEvents b

-- If customer traffic increases, revenue improves
axiom A4 : ∀ b : Bookstore, MoreCustomers b → RevenueImprove b

theorem revenue_conclusion : 
  ¬ OfferGreatService thisBookstore → RevenueImprove thisBookstore := 
fun h => 
  have hEvents : HostEvents thisBookstore := 
    Or.resolve_left (A3 thisBookstore) h
  have hCustomers : MoreCustomers thisBookstore := 
    A2 thisBookstore hEvents
  A4 thisBookstore hCustomers

stage1_iterations.reasoning_content:
We are given premises and a conclusion to formalize.
 Let's break down:

 Premises:
 1. If they offer great customer service, their reputation will grow.
 2. If they host author events, more customers will visit.
 3. The owner either provides excellent service or hosts events.
 4. If customer traffic increases, revenue will improve.

 Conclusion: Given the service quality decreased, did the store's revenue improve?

 However, note: the conclusion is a question. In logical formalization, we often restructure the problem.

 We are to model the situation so that we can eventually prove or disprove the conclusion.
 But the problem says: "did the store's revenue improve?" meaning we are to see if from the premises and the fact that service decreased we can deduce that revenue improved.

 We note that the owner either provides excellent service or hosts events (premise 3). 
 So, if service decreased, that means the owner is not providing excellent service. Therefore, by premise 3, the owner must host events.

 Then, by premise 2: if they host events, more customers will visit.
 And by premise 4: if more customers visit, revenue improves.

 So we would expect that revenue improved.

 However, note: we are only to formalize, not prove. And we must use `sorry`.

 Steps:

 We will need:
 - Types or objects: We have a bookstore. We don't need multiple instances? We can model as propositions.

 Actually, the problem talks about a single bookstore and events happening or not.

 We can model with propositions:

 Let:
   service_great : Prop   (meaning the bookstore offers great customer service)
   reputation_grow : Prop
   host_events : Prop
   more_customers : Prop
   revenue_improve : Prop

 But note: the owner either provides excellent service or hosts events. This is an exclusive or? Actually, the word "either" often in logic is inclusive or, but sometimes exclusive. 1. `NoiseUp` represents "noise levels go up"
2. `PoliceCalled` represents "police are called to intervene"

   - Party causes noise (`Party → NoiseUp`)
   - Drums cause noise (`Drums → NoiseUp`)
   - At least one event occurs (`Party ∨ Drums`)
   - Party noise causes complaints (`Party → Complain`)
   - Complaints cause police intervention (`Complain → PoliceCalled`)

The conclusion "If there was no noise level up, were the police called?" is formalized as `¬NoiseUp → ¬PoliceCalled` since:
- No noise implies no party and no drums (contrapositives of P1,P2)
- No party implies no complaints (contrapositive of P4)
- No complaints implies no police call (contrapositive of P5)

theorem goal : ¬NoiseUp → ¬PoliceCalled := sorry
</lean>

stage1_code:
axiom City : Type

axiom Drought : City → Prop
axiom ImplementRestrictions : City → Prop
axiom RationWater : City → Prop

axiom P1 : ∀ c : City, Drought c → ImplementRestrictions c
axiom P2 : ∀ c : City, Drought c → RationWater c

theorem goal 
  (c : City) 
  (observed : ¬ ImplementRestrictions c ∨ ¬ RationWater c) : 
  ¬ Drought c := 
sorry
stage2_code:
axiom City : Type

axiom Drought : City → Prop
axiom ImplementRestrictions : City → Prop
axiom RationWater : City → Prop

axiom P1 : ∀ c : City, Drought c → ImplementRestrictions c
axiom P2 : ∀ c : City, Drought c → RationWater c

theorem goal 
  (c : City) 
  (observed : ¬ ImplementRestrictions c ∨ ¬ RationWater c) : 
  ¬ Drought c := 
fun hD => 
  match observed with
  | Or.inl hNIR => hNIR (P1 c hD)
  | Or.inr hNRW => hNRW (P2 c hD)

axiom obj : Type

axiom Motivated : obj → Prop

axiom T2 : ∀ x : obj, ImpressesBosses x → Influence x
axiom T3 : (∃ x : obj, ¬ Promotion x) ∨ (∃ x : obj, ¬ Influence x)

theorem goal : Motivated Mark := sorry

stage2_iterations.llm_response:
The theorem `Motivated Mark` cannot be proven from the given axioms. The axioms establish:
- Some employee is ambitious and has the property that if motivated, they work hard (T4).
- Mark impresses his bosses and thus has influence (T_mark, T2).
- There exists an employee without promotion or without influence (T3).

However, there is no axiom linking Mark to ambition or motivation. The ambitious employee in T4 could be someone else, and even if it were Mark, the implication (Motivated Mark → WorkHard Mark) alone does not force Mark to be motivated. A countermodel exists where all axioms hold but Mark is not motivated, confirming the unprovability.

Therefore, the answer is **Uncertain**.

Complete Lean code (with placeholder proof, as the theorem is unprovable):
```lean
axiom obj : Type
axiom Mark : obj

axiom WorkHard : obj → Prop
axiom Promotion : obj → Prop
axiom ImpressesBosses : obj → Prop
axiom Influence : obj → Prop
axiom Motivated : obj → Prop
axiom Ambitious : obj → Prop

axiom T1 : ∀ x : obj, WorkHard x → Promotion x
axiom T2 : ∀ x : obj, ImpressesBosses x → Influence x
axiom T3 : (∃ x : obj, ¬ Promotion x) ∨ (∃ x : obj, ¬ Influence x)
axiom T4 : ∃ x : obj, Ambitious x ∧ (Motivated x → WorkHard x)
axiom T_mark : ImpressesBosses Mark

theorem goal : Motivated Mark := 
  let ⟨x, hx_amb, hx_impl⟩ := T4
  match Classical.em (x = Mark) with
  | Or.inl h_eq => False.elim (by
      rw [h_eq] at hx_impl
      have := hx_impl
      sorry -- Cannot derive contradiction under negation of theorem
      )
  | Or.inr _ => 
      have _ := T_mark
      have _ := T2 Mark T_mark
      sorry -- Unable to link T4 employee to Mark's motivation
```

ANSWER: Uncertain

stage2_iterations.lean_code:
axiom obj : Type
axiom Mark : obj

axiom WorkHard : obj → Prop
axiom Promotion : obj → Prop
axiom ImpressesBosses : obj → Prop
axiom Influence : obj → Prop
axiom Motivated : obj → Prop
axiom Ambitious : obj → Prop

axiom T1 : ∀ x : obj, WorkHard x → Promotion x
axiom T2 : ∀ x : obj, ImpressesBosses x → Influence x
axiom T3 : (∃ x : obj, ¬ Promotion x) ∨ (∃ x : obj, ¬ Influence x)
axiom T4 : ∃ x : obj, Ambitious x ∧ (Motivated x → WorkHard x)
axiom T_mark : ImpressesBosses Mark

theorem goal : Motivated Mark := 
  let ⟨x, hx_amb, hx_impl⟩ := T4
  match Classical.em (x = Mark) with
  | Or.inl h_eq => False.elim (by
      rw [h_eq] at hx_impl
      have := hx_impl
      sorry -- Cannot derive contradiction under negation of theorem
      )
  | Or.inr _ => 
      have _ := T_mark
      have _ := T2 Mark T_mark
      sorry -- Unable to link T4 employee to Mark's motivation
stage2_iterations.full_code:
axiom obj : Type
axiom Mark : obj

axiom WorkHard : obj → Prop
axiom Promotion : obj → Prop
axiom ImpressesBosses : obj → Prop
axiom Influence : obj → Prop
axiom Motivated : obj → Prop
axiom Ambitious : obj → Prop

axiom T1 : ∀ x : obj, WorkHard x → Promotion x
axiom T2 : ∀ x : obj, ImpressesBosses x → Influence x
axiom T3 : (∃ x : obj, ¬ Promotion x) ∨ (∃ x : obj, ¬ Influence x)
axiom T4 : ∃ x : obj, Ambitious x ∧ (Motivated x → WorkHard x)
axiom T_mark : ImpressesBosses Mark

theorem goal : Motivated Mark := 
  let ⟨x, hx_amb, hx_impl⟩ := T4
  match Classical.em (x = Mark) with
  | Or.inl h_eq => False.elim (by
      rw [h_eq] at hx_impl
      have := hx_impl
      sorry -- Cannot derive contradiction under negation of theorem
      )
  | Or.inr _ => 
      have _ := T_mark
      have _ := T2 Mark T_mark
      sorry -- Unable to link T4 employee to Mark's motivation

stage1_code:
axiom obj : Type
axiom Lauren : obj

axiom TrainedHard : obj → Prop
axiom Stretched : obj → Prop
axiom RunRacesFaster : obj → Prop
axiom TookRecoverySeriously : obj → Prop
axiom FocusedOnFlexibility : obj → Prop

-- If trained hard → runs races faster
axiom Premise1 : ∀ (p : obj), TrainedHard p → RunRacesFaster p

-- Trained hard or stretched (or both)
axiom Premise2 : ∀ (p : obj), TrainedHard p ∨ Stretched p

-- Stretching → took recovery seriously
axiom Premise3 : ∀ (p : obj), Stretched p → TookRecoverySeriously p

-- Took recovery seriously → focused on flexibility
axiom Premise4 : ∀ (p : obj), TookRecoverySeriously p → FocusedOnFlexibility p

-- Conclusion: If Lauren doesn't run races faster, she focuses on flexibility
theorem conclusion : ¬ (RunRacesFaster Lauren) → FocusedOnFlexibility Lauren := 
by
  sorry
stage2_code:
axiom obj : Type
axiom Lauren : obj

axiom TrainedHard : obj → Prop
axiom Stretched : obj → Prop
axiom RunRacesFaster : obj → Prop
axiom TookRecoverySeriously : obj → Prop
axiom FocusedOnFlexibility : obj → Prop

-- If trained hard → runs races faster
axiom Premise1 : ∀ (p : obj), TrainedHard p → RunRacesFaster p

-- Trained hard or stretched (or both)
axiom Premise2 : ∀ (p : obj), TrainedHard p ∨ Stretched p

-- Stretching → took recovery seriously
axiom Premise3 : ∀ (p : obj), Stretched p → TookRecoverySeriously p

-- Took recovery seriously → focused on flexibility
axiom Premise4 : ∀ (p : obj), TookRecoverySeriously p → FocusedOnFlexibility p

-- Conclusion: If Lauren doesn't run races faster, she focuses on flexibility
theorem conclusion : ¬ (RunRacesFaster Lauren) → FocusedOnFlexibility Lauren := 
by
  intro h
  cases Premise2 Lauren with
  | inl trained => 
    have : RunRacesFaster Lauren := Premise1 Lauren trained
    contradiction
  | inr stretched =>
    have : TookRecoverySeriously Lauren := Premise3 Lauren stretched
    exact Premise4 Lauren this

To formalize the given problem in Lean 4, we need to represent the premises and the conclusion using axioms and a theorem. The logical structure involves:

1. Defining a type `obj` for individuals (with Lauren as a specific instance)
2. Predicates for training status, recovery habits, speed outcomes, and flexibility goals
3. Premises connecting these predicates
4. The conclusion: If Lauren doesn't run races faster, she focuses on flexibility

Here's the Lean 4 formalization:

<lean>
axiom obj : Type
axiom Lauren : obj

axiom TrainedHard : obj → Prop
axiom Stretched : obj → Prop
axiom RunRacesFaster : obj → Prop
axiom TookRecoverySeriously : obj → Prop
axiom FocusedOnFlexibility : obj → Prop

-- If trained hard → runs races faster
axiom Premise1 : ∀ (p : obj), TrainedHard p → RunRacesFaster p

-- Trained hard or stretched (or both)
axiom Premise2 : ∀ (p : obj), TrainedHard p ∨ Stretched p

-- Stretching → took recovery seriously
axiom Premise3 : ∀ (p : obj), Stretched p → TookRecoverySeriously p

-- Took recovery seriously → focused on flexibility
axiom Premise4 : ∀ (p : obj), TookRecoverySeriously p → FocusedOnFlexibility p

-- Conclusion: If Lauren doesn't run races faster, she focuses on flexibility
theorem conclusion : ¬ (RunRacesFaster Lauren) → FocusedOnFlexibility Lauren := 
by
  sorry
</lean>

This formalization:
- Uses an individual type `obj` and constant `Lauren`
- Defines five predicates corresponding to behavioral and outcome states
- Captures the four premises using universal quantifiers
- Declares the target conclusion as a theorem with `sorry` placeholder
- Maintains exact correspondence with natural language premises through predicate names

We want to prove:

Case 2: Stretched Lauren.
  Then by Premise3, TookRecoverySeriously Lauren.
  Then by Premise4, FocusedOnFlexibility Lauren.

So in the second case we get the desired conclusion.

But note: in the first case we have a contradiction. So we can use proof by cases (or-elimination) and in the first case we can use the contradiction to derive anything (ex falso quodlibet, but in Lean we can use `absurd` or just note that the case is impossible).

We can use the `cases` tactic on Premise2 applied to Lauren.

Alternatively, we can use `have` to get the disjunction and then use `cases` on it.

```
fun h : ¬ RunRacesFaster Lauren => 
```

Now, we have `h : RunRacesFaster Lauren → False`.

We have Premise2 for Lauren: `Premise2 Lauren : TrainedHard Lauren ∨ Stretched Lauren`

We do cases on this:

Case 1: `TrainedHard Lauren`
  Then by Premise1: `Premise1 Lauren : TrainedHard Lauren → RunRacesFaster Lauren`
  So we get `RunRacesFaster Lauren`, which contradicts `h`. So we can use `False.elim` on the contradiction.

Case 2: `Stretched Lauren`
  Then by Premise3: `Premise3 Lauren : Stretched Lauren → TookRecoverySeriously Lauren`
  So we get `TookRecoverySeriously Lauren`
  Then by Premise4: `Premise4 Lauren : TookRecoverySeriously Lauren → FocusedOnFlexibility Lauren`
  So we get `FocusedOnFlexibility Lauren`

We can write this in tactic mode as:

```
theorem conclusion : ¬ (RunRacesFaster Lauren) → FocusedOnFlexibility Lauren := 
by
  intro h  -- h : ¬RunRacesFaster Lauren
  have disj := Premise2 Lauren  -- disj : TrainedHard Lauren ∨ Stretched Lauren
  cases disj with
  | inl trained => 
    -- trained : TrainedHard Lauren
    have runs_faster : RunRacesFaster Lauren := Premise1 Lauren trained
    exact absurd runs_faster h  -- contradiction
  | inr stretched =>
    -- stretched : Stretched Lauren
    have recovery : TookRecoverySeriously Lauren := Premise3 Lauren stretched
    exact Premise4 Lauren recovery
```

Alternatively, we can write it without naming the disjunction:

```

by

  cases Premise2 Lauren with
  | inl trained => 
    have : RunRacesFaster Lauren := Premise1 Lauren trained
    contradiction
  | inr stretched =>
    exact Premise4 Lauren (Premise3 Lauren stretched)
```

The theorem `mark_has_discipline` cannot be proven from the given axioms. Here's the analysis:

1. **Given Axioms**:
   - If a person wakes early, they are energized: `early_energized`
   - If a person plans their day, they are productive: `planned_productive`
   - There exists someone lacking energy or productivity: `some_lacking`
   - If a person has self-discipline, they wake early: `discipline_early`

2. **Mark-Specific Facts**:
   - Mark plans his day (`mark_plans`), so Mark is productive (via `planned_productive`).

3. **Unattainable Proof**:
   - There is no axiom linking "plans day" or "productive" to "self-discipline".
   - The existential (`some_lacking`) does NOT imply anything about Mark, as we don't know who lacks energy/productivity.
   - Mark’s productivity does not force him to be energized or wake early.
   - Without `Energized Mark` or `WakesEarly Mark`, we lack connections to use `discipline_early`.

**Counterexample**: A model exists where Mark:
- Plans his day (productive), 
- Lacks self-discipline (does NOT wake early, NOT energized), 
- While someone else lacks energy/productivity (satisfying `some_lacking`).

Since the theorem is unprovable, we retain `sorry` for the Lean code.

Final ANSWER: **Uncertain** (cannot be proven or disproven from premises).

axiom Person : Type

-- Properties and actions
axiom WakesEarly : Person → Prop
axiom Energized : Person → Prop
axiom PlansDay : Person → Prop
axiom Productive : Person → Prop

-- Premises
axiom early_energized : ∀ p, WakesEarly p → Energized p
axiom planned_productive : ∀ p, PlansDay p → Productive p
axiom some_lacking : ∃ p, ¬Energized p ∨ ¬Productive p
axiom discipline_early : ∀ p, SelfDiscipline p → WakesEarly p

-- Specific individual
axiom Mark : Person
axiom mark_plans : PlansDay Mark

-- Conclusion to examine
theorem mark_has_discipline : SelfDiscipline Mark := sorry
</lean>

axiom planned_productive : ∀ p, PlansDay p → Productive p

stage2_iterations.full_code:
axiom Person : Type

-- Properties and actions
axiom WakesEarly : Person → Prop
axiom Energized : Person → Prop
axiom PlansDay : Person → Prop
axiom Productive : Person → Prop
axiom SelfDiscipline : Person → Prop

-- Premises
axiom early_energized : ∀ p, WakesEarly p → Energized p
axiom planned_productive : ∀ p, PlansDay p → Productive p
axiom some_lacking : ∃ p, ¬Energized p ∨ ¬Productive p
axiom discipline_early : ∀ p, SelfDiscipline p → WakesEarly p

-- Specific individual
axiom Mark : Person
axiom mark_plans : PlansDay Mark

-- Conclusion to examine
theorem mark_has_discipline : SelfDiscipline Mark := sorry

stage1_code:
axiom Clear : Prop          -- Weather clears up
axiom ErrandsDone : Prop    -- Mary finishes her errands
axiom MeetFriends : Prop    -- Mary meets friends for dinner
axiom GameOn : Prop         -- Baseball game goes on
axiom Cheer : Prop          -- Fans get to cheer
axiom ExcitingNight : Prop  -- Exciting night at ballpark

-- If weather clears, game goes on
axiom P1 : Clear → GameOn

-- If errands done, Mary meets friends
axiom P2 : ErrandsDone → MeetFriends

-- Either clear OR errands done (or both)
axiom P3 : Clear ∨ ErrandsDone

-- If game on, fans cheer
axiom P4 : GameOn → Cheer

-- If fans cheer, exciting night
axiom P5 : Cheer → ExcitingNight

-- Conclusion: If Mary didn't meet friends, was it exciting night?
theorem goal : ¬ MeetFriends → ExcitingNight := sorry
stage2_code:
axiom Clear : Prop          -- Weather clears up
axiom ErrandsDone : Prop    -- Mary finishes her errands
axiom MeetFriends : Prop    -- Mary meets friends for dinner
axiom GameOn : Prop         -- Baseball game goes on
axiom Cheer : Prop          -- Fans get to cheer
axiom ExcitingNight : Prop  -- Exciting night at ballpark

-- If weather clears, game goes on
axiom P1 : Clear → GameOn

-- If errands done, Mary meets friends
axiom P2 : ErrandsDone → MeetFriends

-- Either clear OR errands done (or both)
axiom P3 : Clear ∨ ErrandsDone

-- If game on, fans cheer
axiom P4 : GameOn → Cheer

-- If fans cheer, exciting night
axiom P5 : Cheer → ExcitingNight

theorem goal : ¬ MeetFriends → ExcitingNight := by
  intro h
  have h' : ¬ ErrandsDone := fun he => h (P2 he)
  cases P3 with
  | inl c => 
      have g : GameOn := P1 c
      exact P5 (P4 g)
  | inr e => 
      exact False.elim (h' e)
